risk: Unencrypted file transfer – credentials and data in plaintext.
recommendation: Replace FTP with SFTP (port 22) or FTPS.

risk: Plaintext remote access – transmits credentials in clear text.
recommendation: Telnet is obsolete. Use SSH (port 22) instead.

risk: Unencrypted email – potential spam relay and phishing vector.
recommendation: Only open if you run a mail server. Otherwise block.

risk: Windows RPC – frequently targeted for remote exploitation.
recommendation: Do not expose to the internet. Only needed in Windows domains on LAN.

risk: NetBIOS Name Service – legacy protocol, common attack surface.
recommendation: Block on internet-facing interfaces. Legacy protocol – disable if possible.
Block on internet-facing interfaces. Legacy protocol – disable if possible.

risk: NetBIOS Session Service – legacy protocol, SMB vector.
recommendation: Block on internet-facing interfaces. Use SMB over port 445 only.

risk: Windows file sharing – major ransomware vector (EternalBlue).
recommendation: Only open in trusted local networks. Never expose SMB to the internet.

risk: Microsoft SQL Server – database targeted by brute force.
recommendation: Restrict to trusted client IPs. Use VPN for remote access.

risk: Microsoft SQL Server monitor – scanning target.
recommendation: Do not expose publicly. Restrict to trusted clients.
Do not make MySQL publicly accessible. Use localhost or VPN.

risk: Remote Desktop – most common brute-force target.
recommendation: Only use RDP via VPN, or restrict to known IP addresses.
Replace VNC with RDP or use an SSH tunnel.

risk: Redis – often exploited when exposed without authentication.
recommendation: Redis must not be exposed to the internet. Enable authentication.

risk: MongoDB – frequently targeted when exposed to the internet.
recommendation: Do not expose MongoDB publicly. Enable authentication and IP whitelisting.
